\<background\_information\> Automatic differentiation (AD) evaluates the exact partial derivatives of mathematical functions defined by computer programs. For AI agents tasked with instrumenting scientific codebases (PDE solvers, CFD, deep learning), correct AD application requires navigating strict tradeoffs between performance, memory consumption, and mathematical fidelity. This document serves as a condensed, heuristic rulebook to guide autonomous decision-making.1 \</background\_information\>  
Always match the AD tool to the host language and the underlying execution architecture.

| Language Environment | Recommended AD Tool | Agent Action & Integration Strategy |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **C / C++ / Rust** | Enzyme | Operates on LLVM IR post-optimization. Link via CMake (-fplugin=Enzyme.so). Can also be applied directly to IR via opt /path/to/LLVMEnzyme-XX.so \-enzyme inp.ll \-o out.ll. Safely differentiates GPU/CUDA kernels. |
| **Fortran (Legacy)** | Tapenade | Source-to-source translator. Use command-line flags \-d for tangent (forward) mode and \-b for adjoint (reverse) mode. |
| **Fortran (Modern)** | LFortran \+ Enzyme | Compile modern Fortran to LLVM bitcode (.ll), then apply the Enzyme optimization passes. |
| **Python** | JAX / PyTorch | Tracing-based. Refactor dynamic, data-dependent while loops using jax.lax.while\_loop. For custom non-differentiable operations, wrap them using jax.custom\_gradient or jax.custom\_vjp.2 |
| **Julia** | Zygote / Enzyme.jl / ForwardDiff | For forward mode, use ForwardDiff.jl. For reverse mode, use Zygote.jl for pure, vectorized code without mutation (e.g., deep learning). Use Enzyme.jl for scientific machine learning heavily relying on scalar indexing, mutation, and for loops. |

## **2\. Dimensionality Heuristics (Accumulation Modes)**

Evaluate the dimensionality of the input vector ![][image1] and output vector ![][image2] to determine the AD mode.

* **Forward Mode (Tangent):** Enforce when ![][image3] (few inputs, many outputs). Computes the Jacobian-Vector Product (JVP). **Memory footprint:** Extremely low (no tape required).  
* **Reverse Mode (Adjoint):** Enforce when ![][image4] (many inputs, few outputs). Computes the Vector-Jacobian Product (VJP). **Memory footprint:** Extremely high (tape size scales linearly with operations).  
* **Mixed Mode:** Use when computing Hessians or when graph topology exhibits a severe dimensional bottleneck (e.g., mapping large inputs to a small latent space, then back to large outputs). Split the graph and apply reverse mode to the first half and forward mode to the second.

## **3\. "Correct" vs. "Useful" Derivatives (Mathematical Integrity)**

Applying AD literally to implemented code often yields mathematically useless gradients. Scan for and mitigate these architectural anti-patterns:

* **Iterative Solvers & Fixed-Point Loops:** Do NOT apply reverse-mode AD directly through a while loop solver. This unrolls the iteration history, causing out-of-memory (OOM) errors and gradients contaminated by the initial guess. **Fix:** Encapsulate the solver and compute the gradient analytically at the converged steady-state using the Implicit Function Theorem.  
* **Lookup Tables & Discontinuities:** Array indexing and hard cutoffs produce exact zero gradients everywhere except the boundary. **Fix:** Replace discrete table lookups with continuously differentiable interpolants (e.g., Akima or cubic splines).  
* **Step Functions (e.g., ReLU):** In stiff physical equations, ![][image5] discontinuities cause numerical explosions. **Fix:** Replace with smooth analytical approximations like Softplus (![][image6]) using a high inverse temperature ![][image7].

## **4\. Memory Management & Checkpointing**

When utilizing reverse-mode AD on deep computational graphs, the tape will eventually exhaust available RAM/VRAM.

* **Time-Evolution (CFD/PDEs):** Implement **Revolve** (Binomial Checkpointing) to analytically compute the optimal distribution of saved states, minimizing the total number of required recomputations.  
* **Operator Fusion (JAX/PyTorch):** Strategic recomputation of bandwidth-bound pointwise operations (element-wise math, activations) within fused hardware kernels actually reduces *both* peak memory consumption and wall-clock runtime. Enable fusion-aware activation checkpointing aggressively.

## **5\. Fallback Strategies (Opaque/Unsupported Code)**

When encountering legacy dialects or black-box binaries where parsing fails:

1. **Complex-Step Approximation:** Refactor double types to complex. Evaluate ![][image8] with an infinitesimal imaginary step ![][image9]. The exact derivative is ![][image10]. This avoids subtractive cancellation errors entirely. **Constraint:** The codebase must remain strictly complex analytic; operations like complex conjugation or taking absolute values will silently corrupt the gradient without throwing errors.3  
2. **Dual-Number AD (DNAD):** Manually write a Dual struct and overload basic arithmetic operators to implement a bespoke forward-mode engine.  
3. **Finite Differences:** The method of last resort. Susceptible to extreme truncation and roundoff errors.

## **6\. Troubleshooting & Diagnostics**

* **NaN Explosions:** NaN values in gradients cascade and permanently corrupt models. Implement an automated diagnostic hook: on each training step, save a copy of the input batch and stochastic state. Check gradients for NaNs immediately; if detected, halt execution and serialize the clean pre-corruption state to disk for isolated debugging.4  
* **Scalar Bloat:** If tape memory inflates exponentially during tight scalar for loops (especially in Python/Julia), refactor and compress the operations into dense, vectorized matrix algebra.

#### **Works cited**

1. Effective context engineering for AI agents \- Anthropic, accessed February 25, 2026, [https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents)  
2. jax.custom\_gradient \- JAX documentation, accessed February 25, 2026, [https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/\_autosummary/jax.custom\_gradient.html](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/_autosummary/jax.custom_gradient.html)  
3. Complex step differentiation method explained \- Numerics \- Julia Programming Language, accessed February 25, 2026, [https://discourse.julialang.org/t/complex-step-differentiation-method-explained/14647](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/complex-step-differentiation-method-explained/14647)  
4. Debugging the Dreaded NaN \- Towards Data Science, accessed February 25, 2026, [https://towardsdatascience.com/debugging-the-dreaded-nan/](https://towardsdatascience.com/debugging-the-dreaded-nan/)
